viscosity_data.material: Duco Enamel (commercial, as used by Pollock)
viscosity_data.flow_character: Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) — thins under brush load, returns to gel at rest; ideal for drip technique
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Controlled pour at 45°; drip from height forms stable stream before bead breakup
viscosity_data.notes: Pollock preferred Duco brand automotive enamel; also used Krylon and other industrial products. Viscosity optimized for spray/drip, not brush

viscosity_data.flow_character: Bingham plastic — requires yield stress to flow; thixotropic behavior under sustained work; stiff, buttery character
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Does not pour at tube consistency; thinned with mineral spirits to 1000–5000 cP for pour techniques
viscosity_data.notes: Varies considerably by pigment — cadmium, titanium white: very stiff; raw umber, viridian: softer. Linseed oil vs safflower oil affects viscosity and yellowing

viscosity_data.material: Oil Paint — heavily thinned (1:5 paint to mineral spirits)
viscosity_data.flow_character: Near-Newtonian at these dilutions; flows freely; suitable for staining techniques; loses body, pigment concentration reduced
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Excellent pour; spreads on canvas under gravity and capillary action; penetrates raw canvas fibers
viscosity_data.notes: Frankenthaler's early technique (Mountains and Sea) used oil thinned to near-watercolor in turpentine on raw canvas; creates the stain characteristic

viscosity_data.flow_character: Pseudoplastic; designed for controlled pour and flow; consistent viscosity across colors unlike oil
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Good; flows predictably; surface tension creates slight beading at flow front; dries faster limiting flow distance
viscosity_data.notes: Golden Fluid Acrylics and similar products are the modern equivalent for staining/pour techniques; faster drying than oil requires more planned pours

viscosity_data.flow_character: Highly thixotropic; very stiff at rest, shear-thinning under brush; holds peaks in thick application
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Does not pour; equivalent to oil tube consistency for impasto work
viscosity_data.notes: Used for impasto equivalents in acrylic medium; faster drying than oil impasto creates different working character

viscosity_data.material: Watercolor — wet dilution on wet paper
viscosity_data.flow_character: Near-water viscosity; capillary flow dominates on wet paper surface; controlled by paper absorbency and tilt
viscosity_data.pour_behavior: Excellent diffusion; creates soft blooms and wet-into-wet effects; highly surface-condition dependent
viscosity_data.notes: Surface tension of water dominates at this dilution; bloom edges (backruns) form at paint-water boundary

drying_mechanisms.process_description: Unsaturated fatty acid chains in drying oils (linseed, walnut, poppyseed) absorb atmospheric oxygen; oxidation triggers cross-linking polymerization forming a solid polymer network; the process is exothermic and continues in the paint film for decades

stages.state: Skin forms on surface; tacky then firm surface; interior still wet
Paint film no longer moves under light pressure
Full hardness achieved; cross-linking substantially complete
Further cross-linking; slight darkening; increased brittleness in thick passages over decades

oil_comparisons.safflower: Slower, less yellowing — preferred for whites and blues
oil_comparisons.poppyseed: Slowest, least yellowing — traditional for whites

drying_mechanisms.mechanism: Water evaporation followed by polymer coalescence
drying_mechanisms.process_description: Acrylic polymer is held in suspension in water as an emulsion; as water evaporates, polymer particles are drawn together by capillary forces; particles coalesce and fuse into a continuous flexible film above the minimum film-forming temperature (MFFT, typically 15-20°C); process is irreversible once complete

stages.state: Full water content; workable and re-workable
Water evaporating; becoming tacky; mixing colors becomes difficult
Film formed; no longer tacky; can be overworked carefully
Full water evaporation; polymer coalescence complete

factors_affecting_rate: temperature (cold slows dramatically — do not use below 10°C)
drying_mechanisms.critical_temperature: Below 10°C acrylic will not form proper film and will remain powdery or crack when dry

drying_mechanisms.mechanism: Solvent evaporation followed by oxidative polymerization (similar to oil but faster)
drying_mechanisms.process_description: Mineral spirits evaporate first (fast stage); alkyd resin — a synthetic drying oil polyester — then undergoes oxidative polymerization forming a hard, shiny film; alkyds are engineered to dry faster than natural oils

stages.state: Solvent largely evaporated; film firm but not hard
Full chemical cure; maximum hardness and chemical resistance

drying_mechanisms.notes: Commercial enamel films are harder and less flexible than oil or acrylic when cured; risk of cracking in thick applications

surface_interactions.interaction: Oil penetrates fiber; oil binds to cellulose (cotton) or linen fiber; over time, the oil hydrolyzes (breaks down) the cellulose chains, weakening the canvas structurally; acid from oxidizing oil attacks fiber
surface_interactions.visual_effect: Stained appearance; color becomes part of fabric; slight halo at paint boundary
surface_interactions.absorbency: High — oil seeps deeply into fiber matrix
surface_interactions.archival_assessment: Poor for long-term: oil causes fiber degradation; Pollock's raw canvas drip paintings are known conservation concerns
surface_interactions.artist_exploitation: Pollock and some Color Field artists accepted this in exchange for the visual effect

surface_interactions.interaction: Glue seals fiber surface; oil sits in and above glue layer; fiber protected from direct oil contact; size layer can crack in high humidity cycles
surface_interactions.visual_effect: Paint builds as surface layer; slight tooth from canvas texture shows through; color appears slightly more opaque than on raw
surface_interactions.absorbency: Moderate — glue absorbs initial paint before saturation
surface_interactions.archival_assessment: Good with stable environmental conditions; glue re-activates with high humidity
surface_interactions.artist_exploitation: Rothko used sized canvas to control wash absorption — the glue allows wash to spread without full penetration, enabling his layered veil technique

surface_interactions.interaction: Oil paint sits on gesso surface; adhesion relies on mechanical tooth of gesso texture; oil on acrylic can delaminate if oil layers applied too thick and too lean too early
surface_interactions.visual_effect: Paint texture fully visible; bright white ground enhances color luminosity; surface has slight grip
surface_interactions.absorbency: Low to moderate depending on gesso coats
surface_interactions.archival_assessment: Generally good; delamination risk with improperly applied oil over acrylic
surface_interactions.artist_exploitation: Standard practice for post-1960 artists; Mitchell and others used gesso as universal ground for oil and mixed media

surface_interactions.interaction: Acrylic polymer penetrates and binds to fiber; no hydrolysis of fiber; the polymer encapsulates the fiber, protecting rather than degrading it
surface_interactions.visual_effect: Stained effect similar to oil on raw canvas but archivally stable; color absorbed into fiber; characteristic of Color Field second generation
surface_interactions.absorbency: High — thin acrylic absorbed rapidly into raw canvas
surface_interactions.archival_assessment: Good — acrylic does not degrade cotton or linen fiber
surface_interactions.artist_exploitation: Morris Louis, Kenneth Noland from 1953 onward after switching from oil; the stain technique became archivally viable with acrylic

surface_interactions.interaction: Like-on-like adhesion — acrylic on acrylic bonds through polymer fusion; mechanical and chemical adhesion combined
surface_interactions.visual_effect: Paint builds cleanly on white ground; all colors appear accurate; surface can be built to any thickness
surface_interactions.artist_exploitation: Universal standard practice for acrylic-based work

layering_compatibility.description: Matrix indicating compatibility when Layer A (row) is placed over Layer B (column). Values: Excellent, Good, Acceptable, Poor, Not Recommended

Oil (tube) over.Dried acrylic gesso: Good (with careful application, avoid very lean oil first layers)
Oil (tube) over.Dried acrylic paint: Acceptable (mechanical adhesion only; some delamination risk)

Acrylic over.Dried oil: Poor (acrylic will not bond to oil surface)

Watercolor over.Watercolor (on paper): Excellent — glazing is natural to the medium
Watercolor over.Acrylic (on paper): Poor — water beads on acrylic surface

key_rules: Fat over lean (oil): each subsequent oil layer must have higher oil-to-solvent ratio than previous; prevents top layer drying faster and cracking
Acrylic over oil: NOT recommended as acrylic does not bond to oil; reverse order (oil over dried acrylic) is acceptable
Allow adequate drying between layers; wet-into-wet is intentional mixing, not layering
Pollock's mixed-media technique succeeded by applying enamel over enamel over enamel — same medium in successive layers

solvent_effects.effect_on_viscosity: Reduces dramatically — 1:5 dilution creates near-watercolor consistency from tube paint
solvent_effects.effect_on_drying: Faster surface dry as solvent evaporates first; can make paint lean and brittle if overused
solvent_effects.effect_on_color: Slightly desaturates when very dilute; matte effect with high dilution as less oil remains in film
solvent_effects.effect_on_surface: Very thin paint wicks into raw canvas; concentrated areas tend to separate at edges
solvent_effects.artist_use: Pollock used mineral spirits to thin enamel; Frankenthaler used turpentine (similar) for staining medium

solvent_effects.effect_on_viscosity: Similar to mineral spirits but slightly more viscous; dries slower
solvent_effects.effect_on_drying: Slightly slower than mineral spirits; can contribute to yellowing with prolonged exposure
solvent_effects.effect_on_surface: Traditional use for first lean layers and ground preparation
solvent_effects.artist_use: Traditional oil painting solvent; used by Frankenthaler early in career for thinning
solvent_effects.hazards: Flammable; potential allergen; VOC; respiratory hazard

solvent_effects.effect_on_viscosity: Reduces consistently; 1:10 dilution creates watercolor consistency from fluid acrylic
solvent_effects.effect_on_drying: More water = slower evaporation = slower initial drying; but film-forming is still temperature-dependent
solvent_effects.effect_on_color: High dilution reduces pigment concentration; very dilute acrylic approaches watercolor transparency
solvent_effects.effect_on_surface: Very dilute acrylic on raw canvas creates staining comparable to Frankenthaler's technique (used by Color Field second generation)
solvent_effects.artist_use: Universal for water-based media; Morris Louis and Noland used very dilute acrylic on raw canvas post-1953

solvent_effects.effect_on_drying: Slightly faster; resin accelerates surface set
solvent_effects.effect_on_color: Increases saturation and gloss; warm amber tint (slight)
solvent_effects.effect_on_surface: Gloss medium; increases depth and luminosity
solvent_effects.artist_use: Used in lean/fat oil medium mixtures; as final varnish over completed works

absorption_rates.notes: Paint soaks into fiber immediately; no working time for repositioning; edges soft and bleeding; characteristic stain effect results
Linen less absorbent than cotton; tighter weave; slightly more working time; more traditional substrate
Glue layer slows penetration significantly; allows spreading before absorption; ideal for Rothko's wash technique
Some absorption through single coat; greater with more porous gesso formulations
Paint sits on surface; good working time; even surface for controlled mark-making; standard contemporary preparation
Non-absorbent surface; paint slides; extended working time; allows blending; traditional imprimatura effect

effects_on_oil.cold_below_10C: Oil greatly thickens; becomes very difficult to brush; drying slows significantly; outdoor cold-weather painting is challenging
effects_on_oil.warm_above_30C: Oil thins and flows more easily; faster oxidation; Pollock's summer studio work in the Hamptons benefited from warm temperatures increasing flow

effects_on_acrylic.cold_below_10C: CRITICAL: acrylic will not form proper film below its minimum film-forming temperature (MFFT ~10-15°C); dried paint remains powdery and will crack or peel
effects_on_acrylic.warm_above_30C: Faster drying; reduced working time; skin forms rapidly; retarder essential for blending

environmental_factors.effects_on_technique: Pollock's studio in the Hamptons had a tin roof; summer heat dramatically lowered enamel viscosity enabling the characteristic drip quality; winter work required heated studio

effects_on_oil.low_below_30pct: Paint dries faster (humidity affects oxidation rate minimally but lower humidity increases evaporation of solvent components)
effects_on_oil.high_above_80pct: Rabbit skin glue sizing swells dramatically causing canvas to buckle; dried oil paint can show milkiness (bloom); oil grounds expand

effects_on_acrylic.low_below_30pct: Faster drying; surface skin forms rapidly trapping wet paint beneath; can cause cracking in thick applications
effects_on_acrylic.high_above_80pct: Significantly slowed drying; extended working time; beneficial for large-scale pour techniques

environmental_factors.conservation_note: Rothko Chapel paintings suffered from humidity cycling; the rabbit skin glue sizing in his works is particularly sensitive; conservation efforts focus on maintaining stable RH

environmental_factors.effects_on_oil: Accelerates oxidative polymerization (speeds drying initially); long-term causes pigment fading (fugitive pigments), yellowing of oil films, and embrittlement; UV also bleaches some yellowed oil films (museums use controlled UV exposure for restoration)
environmental_factors.effects_on_acrylic: UV causes polymer chain degradation over very long exposure; pigment fading of fugitive colors; less susceptible than oil in general
environmental_factors.museum_standard: 50 lux maximum for light-sensitive works; UV-filtering glass in frames; controlled gallery lighting levels
environmental_factors.historical_note: Pollock stored canvases rolled in his studio; the underside had different UV exposure to the face; conservation assessments note differential aging
